title: Unit 15: The passive, the causative, direct/indirect objects

grammar.usage: Used when the focus is on the action or the object, rather than who performed it. Formed with be + past participle.
grammar.example: Grapefruits are mainly grown in Ninh Thuan province.

grammar.usage: Used to express people's opinions or beliefs, especially in formal or news contexts. Formed with 'It is said that...' or 'N + is said to + infinitive'.
grammar.example: Two people are reported to have been seriously injured.

grammar.usage: Used to show that someone arranges for someone else to do something for them, or that something happens to them. Formed with have/get + object + past participle or get sb to do / have sb do.
grammar.example: We had our herb garden vandalized. / We will have a new cooker put in.

grammar.usage: Verbs with two objects can form two different passive sentences, focusing on either the direct or the indirect object.
grammar.example: My sister was given this cookery book. / This cookery book was given to my sister.

bullets: **Formula:** S + be (in the correct form) + V3/ed (+ by/with + O)

**Usage / When to use:**
* When we do not know who did something
* When it is obvious who did something
* When it is not necessary to know who did something
* When we want to emphasize some new information or use it in formal sentences.

blocks.content: 2. ACTIVE AND PASSIVE FORMS BY TENSE

rows: They mainly grow grapefruits in Ninh Thuan province.

**am/is/are + V3/ed**
Grapefruits are mainly grown in Ninh Thuan province.
Henry is redecorating his bedroom at this moment.

**am/is/are + being + V3/ed**
Henry's bedroom is being redecorated at this moment.

**has/have + been + V3/ed**
Have the potatoes been sliced?

**was / were + V3/ed**
Gunpowder was invented by the Chinese.
We asked for coffee while they were preparing the bill.

**was / were + being + V3/ed**
We asked for coffee while the bill was being prepared.
Someone had broken the flower vase by the time I got there.

**had + been + V3/ed**
The flower vase had been broken by the time I got there.
We will deliver your orders within 50 minutes.

**will + be + V3/ed**
Your orders will be delivered within 50 minutes.
They are going to present the award for best video later tonight.

**is/ are / going to + be + V3/ed**
The award for best video is going to be presented later tonight.
They will have harvested all the bananas by the end of August.

**will + have + been + V3/ed**
All the bananas will have been harvested by the end of August.
You should brush the chicken breast with oil and then fry it.

**modal + be + V3/ed**
The chicken breast should be brushed with oil and then fried.
They should have delivered the groceries by now.

**modal + have + been + V3/ed**
The groceries should have been delivered by now.
Did you hear about them robbing the bank?

**being + V3/ed**
Did you hear about the bank being robbed?
Used when we want to express other people's opinions in news or formal situations.
When a that-clause is used as the object of verbs such as: believe, think, say, report...

bullets: **Structure 1:** It is [said] that + clause
**Structure 2:** N + is/are [said] to + Vbare / (to have V3/ed)

blocks.tableName: Verbs commonly used in Impersonal Passive

bullets:
With verbs like: **argue, suggest, calculate**... → only used in the structure: **"It is [argued/...] that, clause"**
EX: People have suggested that the school should start to produce a magazine.
✗ The school are suggested to should start to produce a magazine.
✓ It has been suggested that the school should start to produce a magazine.

When the action in the that-clause happens **at the same time as / after** the action in the main clause → **to + Vbare**
EX: People think that heart disease is caused by eating the wrong things.
→ Heart disease is thought to be caused by eating the wrong things.
→ It is thought that heart disease is caused by eating the wrong things.
When the action in the that-clause happens **before** the action in the main clause → **to + have + V3/ed**

People have reported that they are involved in human trafficking.
→ It has been reported that they are involved in human trafficking.

People generally think that life won't be found on Mars.
→ It is generally thought that life won't be found on Mars.

They report that two people were seriously injured in the accident. (were injured happens before report)
→ Two people are reported to have been seriously injured in the accident.

People estimated that his restaurant was worth over $10 million. (estimated happens at the same time as was)
→ His restaurant was estimated to be worth over $10 million.
*(Note: textbook uses: His restaurant is estimated to be worth over $10 million.)*

People estimated that his restaurant was worth over $10 million. (estimated happens after was)
→ His restaurant is estimated to have been worth over $10 million.

People say that crocodile tastes like squid. (tastes happens at the same time as say)
→ Crocodile is said to taste like squid.

It is said that the Vikings discovered America before Columbus. (discovered happens before said)
→ The Vikings are said to have discovered America before Columbus.
**Formula:** S1 + have / get (in the correct form) + S2 + V3/ed (+ by/with + O)

**1. Used when we want to express someone's arrangement for S1 related to S2.**
EX: They will put in a new cooker for us next week.
→ We will have a new cooker put in next week.

**2. Expresses an unpleasant situation when something unexpected happens to us.**
EX: We had our herb garden vandalized while we were away.

**3. When we want to express that we arrange for someone to do something for us, we use "get sb to do" or "have sb do".**
EX: Why don't you get the chef to prepare you a vegetarian meal?
OR
EX: Why don't you have the chef prepare you a vegetarian meal?
* **get** is more formal than **have**
* **get sb to do** OR **have sb do** (NOT have sb TO do!)
The subject of a passive sentence can be either the direct or indirect object of the active sentence.

**Active structures:**
* S + V + indirect O + direct O
* S + V + direct O + prep + indirect O

**Key Notes:**
* Indirect Object: usually a person.
* Preposition: usually **to** or **for**.
*(For more details, see Unit 34 in Destination B1)*

EX: A friend gave my sister this cookery book.
→ My sister was given this cookery book by a friend.

EX: A friend gave this cookery book to my sister.
→ This cookery book was given to my sister by a friend.

vocabulary.meaning: give something to a person in authority
vocabulary.example: You must hand in your homework by Friday.

vocabulary.meaning: the force that attracts a body towards the centre of the earth
vocabulary.example: Newton discovered gravity when an apple fell on his head.

vocabulary.meaning: move in a curved path around a star, planet, or moon
vocabulary.example: The Earth orbits the Sun once every year.

vocabulary.meaning: a tool with a heavy metal head used for hitting nails
vocabulary.example: He used a hammer to drive the nail into the wood.

vocabulary.meaning: an act of putting medicine into the body using a syringe
vocabulary.example: The doctor gave the patient an injection of penicillin.

vocabulary.meaning: a medical instrument used to inject or withdraw fluids
vocabulary.example: The nurse carefully loaded the syringe with medicine.

vocabulary.meaning: the law-making assembly of a country
vocabulary.example: The new law was debated in Parliament.

vocabulary.meaning: manage or exploit in a way designed to make a profit
vocabulary.example: They decided to commercialize their new software product.

vocabulary.meaning: a sea animal with a long body and ten tentacles
vocabulary.example: We had grilled squid for dinner at the seaside restaurant.

vocabulary.meaning: a sea animal with a soft body and eight tentacles
vocabulary.example: The octopus hid under a rock when it saw the diver.

vocabulary.meaning: cut hair off the face or another part of the body with a razor
vocabulary.example: He shaves every morning before going to work.

vocabulary.meaning: make a hole in or through something with a sharp point
vocabulary.example: She had her ears pierced when she was twelve.

vocabulary.meaning: cut or damage the surface of something with something sharp
vocabulary.example: The cat scratched the wooden table.
She was furious when she found out they had lied to her.

vocabulary.meaning: corn, a cereal plant that produces large grains on cobs
vocabulary.example: Maize is widely grown in many parts of the world.

vocabulary.meaning: people living in Europe during the Middle Ages
vocabulary.example: Medieval Europeans built many grand cathedrals.
